[[ca:Clan Uesugi]][[de:Uesugi]][[en:Uesugi clan]][[es:Clan Uesugi]][[eu:Uesugi klana]][[fa:خاندان اوئه‌سوگی]][[fr:Clan Uesugi]][[it:Clan Uesugi]][[ja:上杉氏]][[ko:우에스기씨]][[ms:Puak Uesugi]][[pl:Uesugi]][[pt:Clã Uesugi]][[ru:Уэсуги (род)]][[th:ตระกูลอูเอซูงิ]][[tr:Uesugi boyu]][[uk:Рід Уесуґі]][[vi:Gia tộc Uesugi]][[zh:上杉氏]] [[ca:Clan Uesugi]][[de:Uesugi]][[en:Uesugi clan]][[es:Clan Uesugi]][[eu:Uesugi klana]][[fa:خاندان اوئه‌سوگی]][[fr:Clan Uesugi]][[it:Clan Uesugi]][[ja:上杉氏]][[ko:우에스기씨]][[ms:Puak Uesugi]][[pl:Uesugi]][[pt:Clã Uesugi]][[ru:Уэсуги (род)]][[th:ตระกูลอูเอซูงิ]][[tr:Uesugi boyu]][[uk:Рід Уесуґі]][[vi:Gia tộc Uesugi]][[zh:上杉氏]] [[ca:Clan Uesugi]][[de:Uesugi]][[en:Uesugi clan]][[es:Clan Uesugi]][[eu:Uesugi klana]][[fa:خاندان اوئه‌سوگی]][[fr:Clan Uesugi]][[it:Clan Uesugi]][[ja:上杉氏]][[ko:우에스기씨]][[ms:Puak Uesugi]][[pl:Uesugi]][[pt:Clã Uesugi]][[ru:Уэсуги (род)]][[th:ตระกูลอูเอซูงิ]][[tr:Uesugi boyu]][[uk:Рід Уесуґі]][[vi:Gia tộc Uesugi]][[zh:上杉氏]] {{Short description|Japanese clan}} {{more footnotes needed|date=July 2021}} {{family name hatnote|Uesugi|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Uesugi |surname nihongo = 上杉 |image = File:Japanese Crest Uesugi Sasa.svg |image size = 200px |image caption = The emblem ([[Mon (emblem)|''mon'']]) of the Uesugi clan |home province ={{Plainlist| * {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba|1=Tanba Province}} * {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo|1=Echigo Province}} * {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa|1=Dewa Province}}}} |parent house =[[File:Japanese crest Sagari Fuji.svg|15px]] [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) |titles =Various |founder =Uesugi Shigefusa |final ruler = |current head =Uesugi Kuninori |founding year =Late 13th century |dissolution =still extant |ruled until =1868 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches =Ōgigayatsu Uesugi
Inukake Uesugi
Yamanouchi Uesugi }} The {{nihongo|'''Uesugi clan'''|上杉氏|Uesugi-shi, {{ill|Historical kana orthography|en|Historical kana orthography|WD=Q1142552|lt=historically|1=Historical kana orthography}} also '''Uyesugi'''}} is a Japanese samurai clan which was at its peak one of the most powerful during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi|1=Muromachi period}} and {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}s (14th to 17th centuries).{{ill|Georges Appert|en|Georges Appert|WD=Q9289827|lt=Appert, Georges|1=Georges Appert}}. (1888). [https://books.google.com/books?id=CSUNAAAAYAAJ&q=ancien+japon+georges+appert ''Ancien Japon,'' p. 79.] At its height, the clan had three main branches: the Ōgigayatsu, Inukake, and Yamanouchi. Its most well-known member is the warlord {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|1=Uesugi Kenshin}} (1530–1578). During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the Uesugi were a ''[[:en:Tozama|Tozama]]'' or outsider clan, in contrast with the ''[[:en:Fudai|Fudai]]'' or insider ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' clans which had been hereditary vassals or allies of the Tokugawa clan. ==History== The clan claims descent from the [[Fujiwara clan]], specifically {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoshikado|en|Fujiwara no Yoshikado|WD=Q5507548|lt=Fujiwara no Yoshikado|1=Fujiwara no Yoshikado}},{{ill|Edmond Papinot|en|Edmond Papinot|WD=Q4141631|lt=Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph|1=Edmond Papinot}}. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Uesugi", ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 67 [PDF 71 of 80)]]; retrieved 2013-5-11. who was a ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'' during the 9th century. [[Uesugi Shigefusa|Kanjūji Shigefusa]] was a 13th generation descendant of the clan's great progenitor and the originator of the clan's name. Near the end of the 13th century, he received the [[Uesugi domain]] in {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|WD=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province|1=Tango Province}} and adopted the name "Uesugi" after arriving and establishing himself there. ===Muromachi period=== Throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, members of the clan were appointed as ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' (provincial governors) and would also regularly hold sway over the post {{ill|Kanrei|en|Kanrei|WD=Q1202497|lt=Kantō Kanrei|1=Kanrei}} (shogun's deputy in Kantō). Through their dominance of this position, the clan gained a large amount of power in the Kantō region. In 1449, Kantō Kanrei {{ill|Ashikaga Shigeuji|en|Ashikaga Shigeuji|WD=Q4306913|lt=Ashikaga Shigeuji|1=Ashikaga Shigeuji}} killed his Uesugi deputy in order to check the family's power. However, Uesugi forces rose up throughout the region and drove out Shigeuji. After ousting the Kanrei, they asked the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}ate in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} for a different deputy to be placed in power. The Uesugi's deposition of Shigeuji left them as the predominant power in the Kantō region, and the clan was able to grow rapidly. They eventually split into their three main branches, which were named after their traditional homelands. The Ōgigayatsu controlled {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|en|Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1073688|lt=Kawagoe Castle|1=Kawagoe Castle}}, the Yamanouchi were centered in {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Hirai|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}}, and the Inukake held a castle in the region as well. Each of these branch families can trace their lineage to [[Uesugi Kiyoko]], mother of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the founder of the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}}. These three branches would commence infighting for dominance within the clan and the Kantō region almost as soon as the split occurred. This conflict would continue for roughly twenty-five years until around the end of the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} in 1477, which brought the end of the Ashikaga shogunate. Though the Ōgigayatsu and Yamanouchi branches both survived this conflict, the Inukake did not. ===Sengoku period=== Traditionally, the Ōgigayatsu branch allied themselves with the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan|1=Ōta clan}}, while the Yamanouchi were aligned with the {{ill|Nagao clan|en|Nagao clan|WD=Q1655561|lt=Nagao clan|1=Nagao clan}}. While the Ōgigayatsu were less numerous than the Yamanouchi, they held on to power due to {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan|1=Ōta Dōkan}}'s construction of the {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} in the 1450s. At the same time as the Uesugi branches continued to compete for power with themselves, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} began to gain power in the lower area of the Kantō region. The first head of this rising clan, {{ill|Hōjō Sōun|en|Hōjō Sōun|WD=Q924929|lt=Hōjō Sōun|1=Hōjō Sōun}}, allied himself with [[Nagao Tamekage]], Deputy Constable of {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, and would go on to become one of their strongest rivals. This rapid expansion of a rival clan forced the two rival branches of the Uesugi to become allies. In 1537, the city of {{ill|Kawagoe, Saitama|en|Kawagoe, Saitama|WD=Q319740|lt=Kawagoe|1=Kawagoe, Saitama}} fell to the Hōjō clan. By 1545, the united Uesugi launched a campaign to regain their power in the region and retake lost possessions. However, the Ōgigayatsu branch would ultimately come to an end with the death of {{ill|Uesugi Tomosada (16th century)|en|Uesugi Tomosada (16th century)|WD=Q277361|lt=Uesugi Tomosada|1=Uesugi Tomosada (16th century)}} during a failed siege of Kawagoe castle later that year. [[File:Uesugi Kagekatsu.jpg|thumb|154x154px|{{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu|1=Uesugi Kagekatsu}}]] Near the end of the Sengoku period, the Uesugi would undergo major changes in their leadership. {{ill|Uesugi Norimasa|en|Uesugi Norimasa|WD=Q906593|lt=Uesugi Norimasa|1=Uesugi Norimasa}}, holder of a castle which had fallen in 1551 to the Hōjō, took up arms with his retainer [[:en:Nagao Kagetora|Nagao Kagetora]]. Kagetora would go on to change his name to Uesugi Kenshin after campaigning against the Hōjō in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}}. Kenshin would later become one of Sengoku's most prominent generals, continuing to wage war against the Hōjō for control of the Kantō region. Kenshin's adopted son {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu|1=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} eventually became head of the Uesugi clan. However, his support of [[Ishida Mitsunari]] during the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} would result in a devastating blow to the power of the Uesugi, as Mitsunari's forces were crushed by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and his supporters. ===Edo period=== During the Edo period, the Uesugi were given the domain of {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|en|Yonezawa Domain|WD=Q1037948|lt=Yonezawa|1=Yonezawa Domain}}, a ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō|1=Tozama daimyō}}'' worth 300,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}''. The domain, located far from the capital in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}}, was considered fairly representative of what might be given to ''daimyō'' considered "outsiders" by the shogunate. Yonezawa had minimal direct control from the shogunate, but was not urbanized and was largely an agricultural domain. Despite agricultural advances and generally high growth in the 17th century, Yonezawa, like most parts of the country, experienced a considerable drop in growth after 1700. The official ''koku'' revenue of the Uesugi ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' was cut in half in 1664, but the family maintained its same expensive lifestyle as before. After Yonezawa entered debt and experienced famine in the 1750s, the current ''daimyō'' [[Uesugi Shigesada]] considered giving the territory back to the shogunate. Instead, he allowed his adopted son {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|en|Uesugi Harunori|WD=Q1194899|lt=Uesugi Harunori|1=Uesugi Harunori}} to take over as ''daimyō''. Through agricultural and moral reforms, as well as other strict policies, Harunori was able to restore a measure of prosperity to the domain. After his death, the shogunate officially praised Yonezawa as an example of good governance. ===''Bakumatsu''=== When the Uesugi clan faced the threat of abolition due to the sudden death of {{ill|Uesugi Tsunakatsu|en|Uesugi Tsunakatsu|WD=Q8514779|lt=Tsunakatsu|1=Uesugi Tsunakatsu}} (1639–64), they were saved by {{ill|Hoshina Masayuki|en|Hoshina Masayuki|WD=Q837236|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}. In the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} (1868–69), to repay this favor, the Yonezawa Domain rose to assist the {{ill|Aizu Domain|en|Aizu Domain|WD=Q4389944|lt=Aizu Domain|1=Aizu Domain}} in its time of crisis. Alongside the {{ill|Sendai Domain|en|Sendai Domain|WD=Q1062182|lt=Sendai Domain|1=Sendai Domain}} ({{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan|1=Date clan}}), they led the Northern Alliance ({{ill|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|en|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|WD=Q260498|lt=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|1=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei}}) and fought against the new government forces, but after repeated defeats, they eventually surrendered.{{cite web|url=https://www.touken-world.jp/edo-domain100/yonezawa/|title=米沢藩（よねざわはん）[山形県] ／ホームメイト|publisher=Touken World|access-date=10 November 2024}} ===Meiji period and modern era=== [[File:Uesugi Mochinori.jpg|thumb|158x158px|{{ill|Uesugi Mochinori|en|Uesugi Mochinori|WD=Q851787|lt=Uesugi Mochinori|1=Uesugi Mochinori}}]] The [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 brought the abolition of the ''han'' system, that is, the end of the domains, the feudal lords, and the samurai class. During this period, the head of the clan was {{ill|Uesugi Mochinori|en|Uesugi Mochinori|WD=Q851787|lt=Uesugi Mochinori|1=Uesugi Mochinori}}. While the ''han'' system ended, the Uesugi clan survives to this day. Its present head, [[Uesugi Kuninori]] (born 1942), is a professor at the Institute of Space and Astronautical Science, Ministry of Education. ==Crests and banners== The clan crest of the Uesugi was two flying sparrows in bamboo. Uesugi Kenshin had several personal standards: the first character in {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} (毘, bi), a flag of divine appointment, an open fan horse insignia, and the suspended and chaotically written dragon character (龍). ==Notable members and retainers== ===Members=== :{{dynamic list}} {{Div col}} * [[Uesugi Shigefusa]] (13th century) * [[Uesugi Norifusa]] (died 1355) * [[Uesugi Shigeyoshi]] (died 1349) * [[Uesugi Akiyoshi]] (died 1351) * [[Uesugi Yoshinori]] (died 1378) * [[Uesugi Noriharu]] (died 1379) * [[Uesugi Norikata]] (1335–1394) * [[Uesugi Norimoto]] (1383–1418) * {{ill|Uesugi Norizane|en|Uesugi Norizane|WD=Q674269|lt=Uesugi Norizane|1=Uesugi Norizane}} (1410–1466) * [[Uesugi Kiyokata]] (died 1442) * [[Uesugi Fusaaki]] (1432–1466) * [[Uesugi Noritada]] (1433–1454) * {{ill|Uesugi Akisada|en|Uesugi Akisada|WD=Q3547763|lt=Uesugi Akisada|1=Uesugi Akisada}} (1454–1510) * {{ill|Uesugi Tomooki|en|Uesugi [[ca:Clan Uesugi]][[de:Uesugi]][[en:Uesugi clan]][[es:Clan Uesugi]][[eu:Uesugi klana]][[fa:خاندان اوئه‌سوگی]][[fr:Clan Uesugi]][[it:Clan Uesugi]][[ja:上杉氏]][[ko:우에스기씨]][[ms:Puak Uesugi]][[pl:Uesugi]][[pt:Clã Uesugi]][[ru:Уэсуги (род)]][[th:ตระกูลอูเอซูงิ]][[tr:Uesugi boyu]][[uk:Рід Уесуґі]][[vi:Gia tộc Uesugi]][[zh:上杉氏]] [[ca:Clan Uesugi]][[de:Uesugi]][[en:Uesugi clan]][[es:Clan Uesugi]][[eu:Uesugi klana]][[fa:خاندان اوئه‌سوگی]][[fr:Clan Uesugi]][[it:Clan Uesugi]][[ja:上杉氏]][[ko:우에스기씨]][[ms:Puak Uesugi]][[pl:Uesugi]][[pt:Clã Uesugi]][[ru:Уэсуги (род)]][[th:ตระกูลอูเอซูงิ]][[tr:Uesugi boyu]][[uk:Рід Уесуґі]][[vi:Gia tộc Uesugi]][[zh:上杉氏]] [[ca:Clan Uesugi]][[de:Uesugi]][[en:Uesugi clan]][[es:Clan Uesugi]][[eu:Uesugi klana]][[fa:خاندان اوئه‌سوگی]][[fr:Clan Uesugi]][[it:Clan Uesugi]][[ja:上杉氏]][[ko:우에스기씨]][[ms:Puak Uesugi]][[pl:Uesugi]][[pt:Clã Uesugi]][[ru:Уэсуги (род)]][[th:ตระกูลอูเอซูงิ]][[tr:Uesugi boyu]][[uk:Рід Уесуґі]][[vi:Gia tộc Uesugi]][[zh:上杉氏]] {{Short description|Japanese clan}} {{more footnotes needed|date=July 2021}} {{family name hatnote|Uesugi|lang=Japanese}} {{Infobox Japanese clan |surname = Uesugi |surname nihongo = 上杉 |image = File:Japanese Crest Uesugi Sasa.svg |image size = 200px |image caption = The emblem ([[Mon (emblem)|''mon'']]) of the Uesugi clan |home province ={{Plainlist| * {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba|1=Tanba Province}} * {{ill|Echigo Province|en|Echigo Province|WD=Q666514|lt=Echigo|1=Echigo Province}} * {{ill|Dewa Province|en|Dewa Province|WD=Q876032|lt=Dewa|1=Dewa Province}}}} |parent house =[[File:Japanese crest Sagari Fuji.svg|15px]] [[Fujiwara clan]] (藤原氏) |titles =Various |founder =Uesugi Shigefusa |final ruler = |current head =Uesugi Kuninori |founding year =Late 13th century |dissolution =still extant |ruled until =1868 ({{ill|Abolition of the han system|en|Abolition of the han system|WD=Q331905|lt=Abolition of the han system|1=Abolition of the han system}}) |cadet branches =Ōgigayatsu Uesugi
Inukake Uesugi
Yamanouchi Uesugi }} The {{nihongo|'''Uesugi clan'''|上杉氏|Uesugi-shi, {{ill|Historical kana orthography|en|Historical kana orthography|WD=Q1142552|lt=historically|1=Historical kana orthography}} also '''Uyesugi'''}} is a Japanese samurai clan which was at its peak one of the most powerful during the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi|1=Muromachi period}} and {{ill|Sengoku period|en|Sengoku period|WD=Q204023|lt=Sengoku period|1=Sengoku period}}s (14th to 17th centuries).{{ill|Georges Appert|en|Georges Appert|WD=Q9289827|lt=Appert, Georges|1=Georges Appert}}. (1888). [https://books.google.com/books?id=CSUNAAAAYAAJ&q=ancien+japon+georges+appert ''Ancien Japon,'' p. 79.] At its height, the clan had three main branches: the Ōgigayatsu, Inukake, and Yamanouchi. Its most well-known member is the warlord {{ill|Uesugi Kenshin|en|Uesugi Kenshin|WD=Q311080|lt=Uesugi Kenshin|1=Uesugi Kenshin}} (1530–1578). During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the Uesugi were a ''[[:en:Tozama|Tozama]]'' or outsider clan, in contrast with the ''[[:en:Fudai|Fudai]]'' or insider ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' clans which had been hereditary vassals or allies of the Tokugawa clan. ==History== The clan claims descent from the [[Fujiwara clan]], specifically {{ill|Fujiwara no Yoshikado|en|Fujiwara no Yoshikado|WD=Q5507548|lt=Fujiwara no Yoshikado|1=Fujiwara no Yoshikado}},{{ill|Edmond Papinot|en|Edmond Papinot|WD=Q4141631|lt=Papinot, Jacques Edmond Joseph|1=Edmond Papinot}}. (1906). ''Dictionnaire d'histoire et de géographie du Japon''; Papinot, (2003). [http://www.unterstein.net/Toyoashihara-no-Chiaki-Nagaioaki-no-Mitsuho-no-Kuni/NobiliaireJapon.pdf "Uesugi", ''Nobiliare du Japon'', p. 67 [PDF 71 of 80)]]; retrieved 2013-5-11. who was a ''{{ill|Daijō-daijin|en|Daijō-daijin|WD=Q1157586|lt=Daijō-daijin|1=Daijō-daijin}}'' during the 9th century. [[Uesugi Shigefusa|Kanjūji Shigefusa]] was a 13th generation descendant of the clan's great progenitor and the originator of the clan's name. Near the end of the 13th century, he received the [[Uesugi domain]] in {{ill|Tango Province|en|Tango Province|WD=Q1054331|lt=Tango Province|1=Tango Province}} and adopted the name "Uesugi" after arriving and establishing himself there. ===Muromachi period=== Throughout the {{ill|Muromachi period|en|Muromachi period|WD=Q334845|lt=Muromachi period|1=Muromachi period}}, members of the clan were appointed as ''{{ill|Shugo|en|Shugo|WD=Q1141023|lt=Shugo|1=Shugo}}'' (provincial governors) and would also regularly hold sway over the post {{ill|Kanrei|en|Kanrei|WD=Q1202497|lt=Kantō Kanrei|1=Kanrei}} (shogun's deputy in Kantō). Through their dominance of this position, the clan gained a large amount of power in the Kantō region. In 1449, Kantō Kanrei {{ill|Ashikaga Shigeuji|en|Ashikaga Shigeuji|WD=Q4306913|lt=Ashikaga Shigeuji|1=Ashikaga Shigeuji}} killed his Uesugi deputy in order to check the family's power. However, Uesugi forces rose up throughout the region and drove out Shigeuji. After ousting the Kanrei, they asked the {{ill|Shogun|en|Shogun|WD=Q131767|lt=Shogun|1=Shogun}}ate in {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}} for a different deputy to be placed in power. The Uesugi's deposition of Shigeuji left them as the predominant power in the Kantō region, and the clan was able to grow rapidly. They eventually split into their three main branches, which were named after their traditional homelands. The Ōgigayatsu controlled {{ill|Kawagoe Castle|en|Kawagoe Castle|WD=Q1073688|lt=Kawagoe Castle|1=Kawagoe Castle}}, the Yamanouchi were centered in {{ill|Edogawa, Tokyo|en|Edogawa, Tokyo|WD=Q214056|lt=Hirai|1=Edogawa, Tokyo}}, and the Inukake held a castle in the region as well. Each of these branch families can trace their lineage to [[Uesugi Kiyoko]], mother of {{ill|Ashikaga Takauji|en|Ashikaga Takauji|WD=Q297107|lt=Ashikaga Takauji|1=Ashikaga Takauji}}, the founder of the {{ill|Ashikaga shogunate|en|Ashikaga shogunate|WD=Q1154406|lt=Ashikaga shogunate|1=Ashikaga shogunate}}. These three branches would commence infighting for dominance within the clan and the Kantō region almost as soon as the split occurred. This conflict would continue for roughly twenty-five years until around the end of the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}} in 1477, which brought the end of the Ashikaga shogunate. Though the Ōgigayatsu and Yamanouchi branches both survived this conflict, the Inukake did not. ===Sengoku period=== Traditionally, the Ōgigayatsu branch allied themselves with the {{ill|Ōta clan|en|Ōta clan|WD=Q1391922|lt=Ōta clan|1=Ōta clan}}, while the Yamanouchi were aligned with the {{ill|Nagao clan|en|Nagao clan|WD=Q1655561|lt=Nagao clan|1=Nagao clan}}. While the Ōgigayatsu were less numerous than the Yamanouchi, they held on to power due to {{ill|Ōta Dōkan|en|Ōta Dōkan|WD=Q386144|lt=Ōta Dōkan|1=Ōta Dōkan}}'s construction of the {{ill|Edo Castle|en|Edo Castle|WD=Q865913|lt=Edo Castle|1=Edo Castle}} in the 1450s. At the same time as the Uesugi branches continued to compete for power with themselves, the {{ill|Later Hōjō clan|en|Later Hōjō clan|WD=Q431928|lt=Hōjō clan|1=Later Hōjō clan}} began to gain power in the lower area of the Kantō region. The first head of this rising clan, {{ill|Hōjō Sōun|en|Hōjō Sōun|WD=Q924929|lt=Hōjō Sōun|1=Hōjō Sōun}}, allied himself with [[Nagao Tamekage]], Deputy Constable of {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|WD=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}, and would go on to become one of their strongest rivals. This rapid expansion of a rival clan forced the two rival branches of the Uesugi to become allies. In 1537, the city of {{ill|Kawagoe, Saitama|en|Kawagoe, Saitama|WD=Q319740|lt=Kawagoe|1=Kawagoe, Saitama}} fell to the Hōjō clan. By 1545, the united Uesugi launched a campaign to regain their power in the region and retake lost possessions. However, the Ōgigayatsu branch would ultimately come to an end with the death of {{ill|Uesugi Tomosada (16th century)|en|Uesugi Tomosada (16th century)|WD=Q277361|lt=Uesugi Tomosada|1=Uesugi Tomosada (16th century)}} during a failed siege of Kawagoe castle later that year. [[File:Uesugi Kagekatsu.jpg|thumb|154x154px|{{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu|1=Uesugi Kagekatsu}}]] Near the end of the Sengoku period, the Uesugi would undergo major changes in their leadership. {{ill|Uesugi Norimasa|en|Uesugi Norimasa|WD=Q906593|lt=Uesugi Norimasa|1=Uesugi Norimasa}}, holder of a castle which had fallen in 1551 to the Hōjō, took up arms with his retainer [[:en:Nagao Kagetora|Nagao Kagetora]]. Kagetora would go on to change his name to Uesugi Kenshin after campaigning against the Hōjō in {{ill|Sagami Province|en|Sagami Province|WD=Q1053732|lt=Sagami Province|1=Sagami Province}}. Kenshin would later become one of Sengoku's most prominent generals, continuing to wage war against the Hōjō for control of the Kantō region. Kenshin's adopted son {{ill|Uesugi Kagekatsu|en|Uesugi Kagekatsu|WD=Q1376605|lt=Uesugi Kagekatsu|1=Uesugi Kagekatsu}} eventually became head of the Uesugi clan. However, his support of [[Ishida Mitsunari]] during the {{ill|Battle of Sekigahara|en|Battle of Sekigahara|WD=Q234188|lt=Battle of Sekigahara|1=Battle of Sekigahara}} would result in a devastating blow to the power of the Uesugi, as Mitsunari's forces were crushed by {{ill|Tokugawa Ieyasu|en|Tokugawa Ieyasu|WD=Q171977|lt=Tokugawa Ieyasu|1=Tokugawa Ieyasu}} and his supporters. ===Edo period=== During the Edo period, the Uesugi were given the domain of {{ill|Yonezawa Domain|en|Yonezawa Domain|WD=Q1037948|lt=Yonezawa|1=Yonezawa Domain}}, a ''{{ill|Tozama daimyō|en|Tozama daimyō|WD=Q1195912|lt=Tozama daimyō|1=Tozama daimyō}}'' worth 300,000 ''{{ill|Koku|en|Koku|WD=Q855048|lt=Koku|1=Koku}}''. The domain, located far from the capital in the {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|WD=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}}, was considered fairly representative of what might be given to ''daimyō'' considered "outsiders" by the shogunate. Yonezawa had minimal direct control from the shogunate, but was not urbanized and was largely an agricultural domain. Despite agricultural advances and generally high growth in the 17th century, Yonezawa, like most parts of the country, experienced a considerable drop in growth after 1700. The official ''koku'' revenue of the Uesugi ''[[:en:Daimyō|Daimyō]]'' was cut in half in 1664, but the family maintained its same expensive lifestyle as before. After Yonezawa entered debt and experienced famine in the 1750s, the current ''daimyō'' [[Uesugi Shigesada]] considered giving the territory back to the shogunate. Instead, he allowed his adopted son {{ill|Uesugi Harunori|en|Uesugi Harunori|WD=Q1194899|lt=Uesugi Harunori|1=Uesugi Harunori}} to take over as ''daimyō''. Through agricultural and moral reforms, as well as other strict policies, Harunori was able to restore a measure of prosperity to the domain. After his death, the shogunate officially praised Yonezawa as an example of good governance. ===''Bakumatsu''=== When the Uesugi clan faced the threat of abolition due to the sudden death of {{ill|Uesugi Tsunakatsu|en|Uesugi Tsunakatsu|WD=Q8514779|lt=Tsunakatsu|1=Uesugi Tsunakatsu}} (1639–64), they were saved by {{ill|Hoshina Masayuki|en|Hoshina Masayuki|WD=Q837236|lt=Hoshina Masayuki|1=Hoshina Masayuki}}. In the {{ill|Boshin War|en|Boshin War|WD=Q309184|lt=Boshin War|1=Boshin War}} (1868–69), to repay this favor, the Yonezawa Domain rose to assist the {{ill|Aizu Domain|en|Aizu Domain|WD=Q4389944|lt=Aizu Domain|1=Aizu Domain}} in its time of crisis. Alongside the {{ill|Sendai Domain|en|Sendai Domain|WD=Q1062182|lt=Sendai Domain|1=Sendai Domain}} ({{ill|Date clan|en|Date clan|WD=Q1151061|lt=Date clan|1=Date clan}}), they led the Northern Alliance ({{ill|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|en|Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|WD=Q260498|lt=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei|1=Ōuetsu Reppan Dōmei}}) and fought against the new government forces, but after repeated defeats, they eventually surrendered.{{cite web|url=https://www.touken-world.jp/edo-domain100/yonezawa/|title=米沢藩（よねざわはん）[山形県] ／ホームメイト|publisher=Touken World|access-date=10 November 2024}} ===Meiji period and modern era=== [[File:Uesugi Mochinori.jpg|thumb|158x158px|{{ill|Uesugi Mochinori|en|Uesugi Mochinori|WD=Q851787|lt=Uesugi Mochinori|1=Uesugi Mochinori}}]] The [[Meiji Restoration]] in 1868 brought the abolition of the ''han'' system, that is, the end of the domains, the feudal lords, and the samurai class. During this period, the head of the clan was {{ill|Uesugi Mochinori|en|Uesugi Mochinori|WD=Q851787|lt=Uesugi Mochinori|1=Uesugi Mochinori}}. While the ''han'' system ended, the Uesugi clan survives to this day. Its present head, [[Uesugi Kuninori]] (born 1942), is a professor at the Institute of Space and Astronautical Science, Ministry of Education. ==Crests and banners== The clan crest of the Uesugi was two flying sparrows in bamboo. Uesugi Kenshin had several personal standards: the first character in {{ill|Vaiśravaṇa|en|Vaiśravaṇa|WD=Q866315|lt=Bishamonten|1=Vaiśravaṇa}} (毘, bi), a flag of divine appointment, an open fan horse insignia, and the suspended and chaotically written dragon character (龍). ==Notable members and retainers== ===Members=== :{{dynamic list}} {{Div col}} * [[Uesugi Shigefusa]] (13th century) * [[Uesugi
